{{Original research|date=April 2023}}
In [[molecular biology]], '''competing endogenous RNAs''' (abbreviated '''''ceRNAs''''') regulate other RNA transcripts by competing for shared [[microRNA]]s (miRNAs).<ref name="pmid21802130">{{cite journal | vauthors = Salmena L, Poliseno L, Tay Y, Kats L, Pandolfi PP | title = A ceRNA hypothesis: the Rosetta Stone of a hidden RNA language? | journal = Cell | volume = 146 | issue = 3 | pages = 353–8 | date = August 2011 | pmid = 21802130 | pmc = 3235919 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2011.07.014 }}</ref> Models for ceRNA regulation describe how changes in the expression of one or multiple miRNA targets alter the number of unbound miRNAs and lead to observable changes in miRNA activity - i.e., the abundance of other miRNA targets. Models of ceRNA regulation differ greatly. Some describe the kinetics of target-miRNA-target interactions, where changes in the expression of one target species sequester one miRNA species and lead to changes in the dysregulation of the other target species. Others attempt to model more realistic cellular scenarios, where multiple RNA targets are affecting multiple miRNAs and where each target pair is co-regulated by multiple miRNA species.<ref name=":2" /> Some models focus on [[3'-UTR|mRNA]] 3' UTRs as targets, and others consider [[long non-coding RNA]] targets as well.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3">{{cite journal | vauthors = Chiu HS, Somvanshi S, Patel E, Chen TW, Singh VP, Zorman B, Patil SL, Pan Y, Chatterjee SS, Sood AK, Gunaratne PH, Sumazin P | title = Pan-Cancer Analysis of lncRNA Regulation Supports Their Targeting of Cancer Genes in Each Tumor Context | journal = Cell Reports | volume = 23 | issue = 1 | pages = 297–312.e12 | date = April 2018 | pmid = 29617668 | pmc = 5906131 | doi = 10.1016/j.celrep.2018.03.064 }}</ref><ref name=":4" />

Hundreds of publications have described the influence of ceRNA regulation in normal and disease cells, but ceRNA regulation and its effects continue to be debated in scientific circles.

== Summary ==
[[MicroRNAs]]  are an abundant class of small, non-coding [[RNAs]] (~22nt long), which negatively regulate [[gene expression]] at the levels of [[messenger RNA]]s (mRNAs) stability and translation inhibition. The [[human genome]] contains over 1000 miRNAs, each one targeting hundreds of different genes. It is estimated that half of all genes of the genome are targets of miRNA, spanning a large layer of regulation on a post-transcriptional level.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Friedman RC, Farh KK, Burge CB, Bartel DP | title = Most mammalian mRNAs are conserved targets of microRNAs | journal = Genome Research | volume = 19 | issue = 1 | pages = 92–105 | date = January 2009 | pmid = 18955434 | pmc = 2612969 | doi = 10.1101/Gr.082701.108 }}</ref> The seed region, which comprises nucleotides 2-8 of the 5' portion of the miRNA, is particularly crucial for mRNA recognition and silencing.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Lewis BP, Burge CB, Bartel DP | title = Conserved seed pairing, often flanked by adenosines, indicates that thousands of human genes are microRNA targets | journal = Cell | volume = 120 | issue = 1 | pages = 15–20 | date = January 2005 | pmid = 15652477 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2004.12.035 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Recent studies have shown that the interaction of the miRNA seed region with mRNA is not unidirectional, but that the pool of mRNAs, transcribed [[pseudogenes]], long noncoding RNAs ([[lncRNA]]),<ref name=":1">{{cite journal | vauthors = Cesana M, Cacchiarelli D, Legnini I, Santini T, Sthandier O, Chinappi M, Tramontano A, Bozzoni I | title = A long noncoding RNA controls muscle differentiation by functioning as a competing endogenous RNA | journal = Cell | volume = 147 | issue = 2 | pages = 358–69 | date = October 2011 | pmid = 22000014 | pmc = 3234495 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2011.09.028 }}</ref> [[circular RNA]] (circRNA) <ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hansen TB, Jensen TI, Clausen BH, Bramsen JB, Finsen B, Damgaard CK, Kjems J | title = Natural RNA circles function as efficient microRNA sponges | journal = Nature | volume = 495 | issue = 7441 | pages = 384–8 | date = March 2013 | pmid = 23446346 | doi = 10.1038/nature11993 | bibcode = 2013Natur.495..384H | s2cid = 205233122 }}</ref><ref name=":4">{{cite journal | vauthors = Memczak S, Jens M, Elefsinioti A, Torti F, Krueger J, Rybak A, Maier L, Mackowiak SD, Gregersen LH, Munschauer M, Loewer A, Ziebold U, Landthaler M, Kocks C, le Noble F, Rajewsky N | title = Circular RNAs are a large class of animal RNAs with regulatory potency | journal = Nature | volume = 495 | issue = 7441 | pages = 333–8 | date = March 2013 | pmid = 23446348 | doi = 10.1038/nature11928 | bibcode = 2013Natur.495..333M | s2cid = 4416605 }}</ref> compete for the same pool of miRNA thereby regulating miRNA activity.<ref name=tay /> These competitive endogenous RNAs (ceRNAs) act as molecular sponges for a microRNA through their miRNA binding sites (also referred to as miRNA response elements, MRE), thereby de-repressing all target genes of the respective miRNA family. Experimental evidence for such a ceRNA crosstalk has been initially shown for the tumor suppressor gene PTEN, which is regulated by the 3' [[untranslated region]] ([[3'UTR]]) of the [[pseudogene]] PTENP1 in a [[DICER]]-dependent manner.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{cite journal | vauthors = Poliseno L, Salmena L, Zhang J, Carver B, Haveman WJ, Pandolfi PP | title = A coding-independent function of gene and pseudogene mRNAs regulates tumour biology | journal = Nature | volume = 465 | issue = 7301 | pages = 1033–8 | date = June 2010 | pmid = 20577206 | pmc = 3206313 | doi = 10.1038/nature09144 | bibcode = 2010Natur.465.1033P }}</ref>

A new mechanism has recently been shown in which two closely spaced MREs (of the same or of different miRNA families) can cooperatively sequester miRNAs and thereby significantly boost a ceRNA effect.<ref name="Denzler et al., Mol Cell, 2016">{{cite journal | vauthors = Denzler R, McGeary SE, Title AC, Agarwal V, Bartel DP, Stoffel M | title = Impact of MicroRNA Levels, Target-Site Complementarity, and Cooperativity on Competing Endogenous RNA-Regulated Gene Expression | journal = Molecular Cell | volume = 64 | issue = 3 | pages = 565–579 | date = November 2016 | pmid = 27871486 | pmc = 5101187 | doi = 10.1016/j.molcel.2016.09.027 }}</ref> For a cooperative effect to be considered two adjacent MREs, however, have to be of miRNA families that are expressed high enough to actively repress targets and to be less than 58 [[nucleotides]] apart.{{Cn|date=April 2023}}

The biological relevance of the ceRNA hypothesis has been actively debated. Most notably, it has been challenged by a group of researchers that performed a quantitative assessment of two miRNA families (highly and lowly expressed) and their binding sites in liver- and embryonic stem cells as described below.<ref name="Denzler et al., Mol Cell, 2016" /><ref name="Denzler et al., Mol Cell, 2014">{{cite journal | vauthors = Denzler R, Agarwal V, Stefano J, Bartel DP, Stoffel M | title = Assessing the ceRNA hypothesis with quantitative measurements of miRNA and target abundance | journal = Molecular Cell | volume = 54 | issue = 5 | pages = 766–76 | date = June 2014 | pmid = 24793693 | pmc = 4267251 | doi = 10.1016/j.molcel.2014.03.045 }}</ref> However, these studies were focused on one miRNA per context, and their leading researchers later identified physiologically-relevant ceRNA regulation of another miRNA.<ref name=":5">{{cite journal | vauthors = Kleaveland B, Shi CY, Stefano J, Bartel DP | title = A Network of Noncoding Regulatory RNAs Acts in the Mammalian Brain | journal = Cell | volume = 174 | issue = 2 | pages = 350–362.e17 | date = June 2018 | pmid = 29887379 | pmc = 6559361 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2018.05.022 }}</ref>

== Debate on physiological relevance ==
Two studies by Bosson et al. (2014) and Denzler et al. (2014) have empirically assessed the ceRNA hypothesis by quantifying the number MREs that must be added to detect ceRNA-mediated gene regulation.<ref name="Denzler et al., Mol Cell, 2014" /><ref name="Bosson et al.">{{cite journal | vauthors = Bosson AD, Zamudio JR, Sharp PA | title = Endogenous miRNA and target concentrations determine susceptibility to potential ceRNA competition | journal = Molecular Cell | volume = 56 | issue = 3 | pages = 347–59 | date = November 2014 | pmid = 25449132 | pmc = 5048918 | doi = 10.1016/j.molcel.2014.09.018 }}</ref> Both studies agree that determining the number of transcriptomic miRNA-binding sites is crucial for evaluating the potential for ceRNA regulation and that miRNA binding sites are generally higher than the number of miRNA molecules. However, they differ in two aspects: (1) the experimental approaches used to determine the number of ''effective'' transcriptomic miRNA-binding sites and (2) the impact miRNA concentrations have on the number of binding sites that must be added to detect target gene derepression. The discrepancies between these studies lead to different conclusions with respect to the likelihood of observing ceRNA effects in natural settings, with Bosson et al. observing a ceRNA effect at physiologically plausible and Denzler et al. at unphysiological competitor levels.{{Cn|date=April 2023}}

In a later study, Denzler et al. (2016) has revisited the discrepancies between the two studies and has shown that while miRNA levels define the extent of repression, they have little effect on the number of binding sites that need to be added to observe ceRNA-mediated regulation.<ref name="Denzler et al., Mol Cell, 2016" /> Using the same cells and experimental systems as the two studies they suggested that the number of binding sites are very high and better reflects the estimates of the study by Denzler et al. (2014), and that low-affinity/background miRNA sites (such as 6-nt sites, offset 6-nt sites, non-canonical sites) significantly contribute to competition.<ref name="Denzler et al., Mol Cell, 2016" /> Due to this large number of background sites, their model suggests that prospects of observing an effect from a ceRNA are greatly reduced. Bosia et al. used single-cell assays to demonstrate substantial ceRNA crosstalk in instances where there is a balance between binding site counts, miRNAs, and target RNA expression profiles.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bosia C, Sgrò F, Conti L, Baldassi C, Brusa D, Cavallo F, Cunto FD, Turco E, Pagnani A, Zecchina R | title = RNAs competing for microRNAs mutually influence their fluctuations in a highly non-linear microRNA-dependent manner in single cells | journal = Genome Biology | volume = 18 | issue = 1 | article-number = 37 | date = February 2017 | pmid = 28219439 | pmc = 5319025 | doi = 10.1186/s13059-017-1162-x | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Opponents of the ceRNA hypothesis pointed out that irrefutable proof of ceRNA-mediated gene regulation still remains to be shown since most studies either overexpress RNA transcripts at unphysiological levels <ref name="Jeyapalan" /><ref name="Lee" /> or lack seed mutation controls when up- or down-regulating potential ceRNA transcripts.<ref name="pmid21802130" /><ref name="Karreth" /> A mechanistically elegant study is especially important, as supporters argue that the quantity of work alone is in favor the ceRNA hypothesis. Two recent studies resolved this issue, demonstrating physiological effects and site-specific effects for ceRNA regulation.<ref name=":2">{{cite journal | vauthors = Chiu HS, Martínez MR, Komissarova EV, Llobet-Navas D, Bansal M, Paull EO, Silva J, Yang X, Sumazin P, Califano A | title = The number of titrated microRNA species dictates ceRNA regulation | journal = Nucleic Acids Research | volume = 46 | issue = 9 | pages = 4354–4369 | date = May 2018 | pmid = 29684207 | pmc = 5961349 | doi = 10.1093/nar/gky286 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Piwecka M, Glažar P, Hernandez-Miranda LR, Memczak S, Wolf SA, Rybak-Wolf A, Filipchyk A, Klironomos F, Cerda Jara CA, Fenske P, Trimbuch T, Zywitza V, Plass M, Schreyer L, Ayoub S, Kocks C, Kühn R, Rosenmund C, Birchmeier C, Rajewsky N | display-authors = 6 | title = Loss of a mammalian circular RNA locus causes miRNA deregulation and affects brain function | journal = Science | volume = 357 | issue = 6357 | article-number = eaam8526 | date = September 2017 | pmid = 28798046 | doi = 10.1126/science.aam8526 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Supporters of the ceRNA hypothesis criticized the studies by Denzler et al. for their focus on competition for a single miRNA. They argue that since ceRNA regulations are orchestrated through the cooperative effect of multiple miRNA families,<ref name="Chiu et. al, Genome Res 2015" /> the study by Denzler et al. does not represent a typical ceRNA competitor and can therefore not be used to generalize. In addition, supporters are not surprised that our mechanistic understanding of ceRNA regulation remains incomplete. Instead, they point out that hundreds of genetics and molecular biology studies have found ceRNA regulation physiologically relevant.{{Cn|date=April 2023}}

== Experimentally validated regulators and networks ==

=== High-throughput validation of ceRNA regulatory networks ===
Chiu et al. used LINCS data to support the regulation of hundreds of genes by ceRNA interactions in prostate and breast adenocarcinomas.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Chiu HS, Martínez MR, Bansal M, Subramanian A, Golub TR, Yang X, Sumazin P, Califano A | title = High-throughput validation of ceRNA regulatory networks | journal = BMC Genomics | volume = 18 | issue = 1 | article-number = 418 | date = May 2017 | pmid = 28558729 | pmc = 5450082 | doi = 10.1186/s12864-017-3790-7 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

=== PTEN ceRNA Network ===
[[PTEN (gene)|PTEN]] is a critical [[tumor suppressor gene]] which is frequently altered in multiple human cancers and is a negative regulator of the oncogenic [[Phosphoinositide 3-kinase]]/[[Akt]] signaling pathway. Three studies have identified and successfully validated protein-coding transcripts as PTEN ceRNAs in prostate cancer,<ref name="tay">{{cite journal | vauthors = Tay Y, Kats L, Salmena L, Weiss D, Tan SM, Ala U, Karreth F, Poliseno L, Provero P, Di Cunto F, Lieberman J, Rigoutsos I, Pandolfi PP | title = Coding-independent regulation of the tumor suppressor PTEN by competing endogenous mRNAs | journal = Cell | volume = 147 | issue = 2 | pages = 344–57 | date = October 2011 | pmid = 22000013 | pmc = 3235920 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2011.09.029 }}</ref> [[glioblastoma]]<ref name="Sumazin, P. 2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Sumazin P, Yang X, Chiu HS, Chung WJ, Iyer A, Llobet-Navas D, Rajbhandari P, Bansal M, Guarnieri P, Silva J, Califano A | title = An extensive microRNA-mediated network of RNA-RNA interactions regulates established oncogenic pathways in glioblastoma | journal = Cell | volume = 147 | issue = 2 | pages = 370–81 | date = October 2011 | pmid = 22000015 | pmc = 3214599 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2011.09.041 }}</ref> and melanoma.<ref name="kar">{{cite journal | vauthors = Karreth FA, Tay Y, Perna D, Ala U, Tan SM, Rust AG, DeNicola G, Webster KA, Weiss D, Perez-Mancera PA, Krauthammer M, Halaban R, Provero P, Adams DJ, Tuveson DA, Pandolfi PP | title = In vivo identification of tumor- suppressive PTEN ceRNAs in an oncogenic BRAF-induced mouse model of melanoma | journal = Cell | volume = 147 | issue = 2 | pages = 382–95 | date = October 2011 | pmid = 22000016 | pmc = 3236086 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2011.09.032 }}</ref> PTEN ceRNAs CNOT6L, VAPA and [[ZEB2]] have been shown to regulate PTEN expression, PI3K signaling, and cell proliferation in a 3'UTR- and microRNA-dependent manner.<ref name="tay"/><ref name="kar"/> Similarly, in glioblastoma, siRNA-mediated silencing of 13 predicted PTEN ceRNAs including [[Retinoblastoma protein]] (RB1), [[RUNX1]] and VEGFA downregulated PTEN expression in a 3'UTR-dependent manner and increased tumor [[cell growth]].<ref name="Sumazin, P. 2011"/> However, a replication effort of the initial prostate cancer study found that many of the results could not be replicated, and that many of the experimental interventions had no effect, or the opposite effect of what was originally reported.<ref name="wang">{{cite journal | vauthors = Wang H, Radomska HS, Phelps MA, ((Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology)) | title = Replication Study: Coding-independent regulation of the tumor suppressor PTEN by competing endogenous mRNAs | journal = eLife | issue = 2020;9:e56651 | date = October 2020 | volume = 9 | article-number = e56651 | pmid = 33073769 | pmc = 7572125 | doi = 10.7554/eLife.56651 | doi-access = free }}</ref> 

Additionally, PTEN's non protein-coding pseudogene, [[PTENP1]], is able to affect PTEN expression, downstream PI3K signaling and cell proliferation by directly competing for PTEN-targeting microRNAs.<ref name="ReferenceA"/>

===Linc-MD1===
Linc-MD1, a muscle-specific long non-coding RNA, activates muscle-specific gene expression by regulating expression of MAML1 and MEF2C via antagonizing miR-133 and miR-135.<ref name=":1"/> Whether Linc-MD1 regulates miRNA activity by sequestering miRNA through a typical ceRNA mechanism or if the highly complementary miR-133 site regulates miRNA activity through target-directed degradation remains to be shown.{{Cn|date=April 2023}}

=== BRAFP1 ===
BRAFP1, the [[BRAF (gene)]] pseudogene, has been implicated in the development of cancer, including [[B-cell lymphoma]], by acting as a ceRNA for BRAF. Upregulation of BRAFP1 led to an overexpression of the BRAF [[oncogene]].<ref name="Karreth">{{cite journal | vauthors = Karreth FA, Reschke M, Ruocco A, Ng C, Chapuy B, Léopold V, Sjoberg M, Keane TM, Verma A, Ala U, Tay Y, Wu D, Seitzer N, Velasco-Herrera M, Bothmer A, Fung J, Langellotto F, Rodig SJ, Elemento O, Shipp MA, Adams DJ, Chiarle R, Pandolfi PP | display-authors = 6 | title = The BRAF pseudogene functions as a competitive endogenous RNA and induces lymphoma in vivo | journal = Cell | volume = 161 | issue = 2 | pages = 319–32 | date = April 2015 | pmid = 25843629 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2015.02.043 | pmc = 6922011 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

=== Hepatitis C virus (HCV)===
[[Hepatitis C]] has shown been suggested to regulate [[miR-122]] through be a ceRNA mechanism when overexpressed in Huh-7.5 cells.<ref name=":0">{{cite journal | vauthors = Luna JM, Scheel TK, Danino T, Shaw KS, Mele A, Fak JJ, Nishiuchi E, Takacs CN, Catanese MT, de Jong YP, Jacobson IM, Rice CM, Darnell RB | title = Hepatitis C virus RNA functionally sequesters miR-122 | journal = Cell | volume = 160 | issue = 6 | pages = 1099–110 | date = March 2015 | pmid = 25768906 | pmc = 4386883 | doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2015.02.025 }}</ref> It however still remains to be shown whether Hepatitis C can reach the high titers necessary [[in vivo]] in order to modulate gene expression through a ceRNA mechanism.{{Cn|date=April 2023}}

===KRAS1P===
Another pseudogene shown to have ceRNA activity is that of the proto-oncogene [[KRAS]], KRAS1P, which increases KRAS transcript abundance and accelerates cell growth.<ref name="ReferenceA"/>

===CD44===
The [[CD44]] 3'UTR has been shown to regulate expression of the CD44 protein and [[cell cycle]] regulation protein, [[CDC42]], by antagonizing the function of three microRNAs - miR-216, miR-330 and miR-608.<ref name="Jeyapalan">{{cite journal | vauthors = Jeyapalan Z, Deng Z, Shatseva T, Fang L, He C, Yang BB | title = Expression of CD44 3'-untranslated region regulates endogenous microRNA functions in tumorigenesis and angiogenesis | journal = Nucleic Acids Research | volume = 39 | issue = 8 | pages = 3026–41 | date = April 2011 | pmid = 21149267 | pmc = 3082902 | doi = 10.1093/nar/gkq1003 }}</ref>

===Versican===
The [[versican]] 3'UTR has been shown to regulate expression of the matrix protein [[fibronectin]] via antagonizing miR-199a function.<ref name="Lee">{{cite journal | vauthors = Lee DY, Jeyapalan Z, Fang L, Yang J, Zhang Y, Yee AY, Li M, Du WW, Shatseva T, Yang BB | title = Expression of versican 3'-untranslated region modulates endogenous microRNA functions | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 5 | issue = 10 | article-number = e13599 | date = October 2010 | pmid = 21049042 | pmc = 2963607 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0013599 | editor1-last = Najbauer | bibcode = 2010PLoSO...513599L | editor1-first = Joseph | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Lee DY, Shatseva T, Jeyapalan Z, Du WW, Deng Z, Yang BB | title = A 3'-untranslated region (3'UTR) induces organ adhesion by regulating miR-199a* functions | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 4 | issue = 2 | article-number = e4527 | year = 2009 | pmid = 19223980 | pmc = 2638016 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0004527 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2009PLoSO...4.4527L }}</ref>

===HSUR 1, 2===
T cells transformed by the primate virus ''[[Herpesvirus saimiri]]'' (HVS) have been shown to express viral U-rich noncoding RNAs called [[HSUR]]s. Several of these HSURs are able to bind to and compete for three host-cell microRNAs and thus regulate host-cell gene expression.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Cazalla D, Yario T, Steitz JA, Steitz J | title = Down-regulation of a host microRNA by a Herpesvirus saimiri noncoding RNA | journal = Science | volume = 328 | issue = 5985 | pages = 1563–6 | date = June 2010 | pmid = 20558719 | pmc = 3075239 | doi = 10.1126/science.1187197 | bibcode = 2010Sci...328.1563C }}</ref>

===ESR1===
ESR1 has been shown to be regulated by multiple miRNAs that are highly expressed in ER-negative breast cancer, and its 3' UTR was shown to regulate and be regulated by 3' UTRs of ''CCND1'', ''HIF1A'' and ''NCOA3''.<ref name="Chiu et. al, Genome Res 2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Chiu HS, Llobet-Navas D, Yang X, Chung WJ, Ambesi-Impiombato A, Iyer A, Kim HR, Seviour EG, Luo Z, Sehgal V, Moss T, Lu Y, Ram P, Silva J, Mills GB, Califano A, Sumazin P | title = Cupid: simultaneous reconstruction of microRNA-target and ceRNA networks | journal = Genome Research | volume = 25 | issue = 2 | pages = 257–67 | date = February 2015 | pmid = 25378249 | pmc = 4315299 | doi = 10.1101/gr.178194.114 }}</ref>

=== MYCN ===
MYCN amplification in neuroblastoma has been shown to deplete the abundance of its miRNA regulators, supporting MYCN's role as a master ceRNA regulator in neuroblastoma.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Powers JT, Tsanov KM, Pearson DS, Roels F, Spina CS, Ebright R, Seligson M, de Soysa Y, Cahan P, Theißen J, Tu HC, Han A, Kurek KC, LaPier GS, Osborne JK, Ross SJ, Cesana M, Collins JJ, Berthold F, Daley GQ | title = Multiple mechanisms disrupt the let-7 microRNA family in neuroblastoma | journal = Nature | volume = 535 | issue = 7611 | pages = 246–51 | date = July 2016 | pmid = 27383785 | pmc = 4947006 | doi = 10.1038/nature18632 | bibcode = 2016Natur.535..246P }}</ref>

=== HULC ===
Highly Up-regulated in liver cancer (HULC) is one of the most upregulated of all genes in hepatocellular carcinoma. [[CREB]] (cAMP response element binding protein) has been implicated in the upregulation of HULC.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wang J, Liu X, Wu H, Ni P, Gu Z, Qiao Y, Chen N, Sun F, Fan Q | title = CREB up-regulates long non-coding RNA, HULC expression through interaction with microRNA-372 in liver cancer | journal = Nucleic Acids Research | volume = 38 | issue = 16 | pages = 5366–83 | date = September 2010 | pmid = 20423907 | pmc = 2938198 | doi = 10.1093/nar/gkq285 }}</ref> HULC RNA inhibits miR-372 activity through a ceRNA function, leading to derepression of one of its target genes, [[PRKACB]], which can then induce the phosphorylation and activation of CREB. Overall, HULC lncRNA is part of a self-amplifying autoregulatory loop in which it sponges miR-372 to activate CREB, and in turn upregulates its own expression levels.{{Cn|date=April 2023}}

=== ceRNA in bacteria ===
[[Bacteria]] do not have miRNA, and instead, ceRNAs in these organisms compete for [[Small RNA|small RNAs (sRNAs)]] or [[RNA-binding protein|RNA-binding proteins (RBPs)]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bossi L, Figueroa-Bossi N | title = Competing endogenous RNAs: a target-centric view of small RNA regulation in bacteria | journal = Nature Reviews. Microbiology | volume = 14 | issue = 12 | pages = 775–784 | date = December 2016 | pmid = 27640758 | doi = 10.1038/nrmicro.2016.129 | s2cid = 205496765 }}</ref> Similarly, competition by ceRNAs for RNA-binding proteins has also been reported in eukaryotic cells.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Karreth FA, Tay Y, Pandolfi PP | title = Target competition: transcription factors enter the limelight | journal = Genome Biology | volume = 15 | issue = 4 | article-number = 114 | date = April 2014 | pmid = 25001290 | pmc = 4052382 | doi = 10.1186/gb4174 | doi-access = free }}</ref>
